summary: Skeleton3D consumes a large amount of CPU when moving even when hidden by visibility parent or visibility range

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in Godot 4.5-beta3

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta3 - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 Ti Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7628) - 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-12800H (20 threads) - 15.69 GiB memory

### Issue description

Skeleton3D consumes a very large amount of CPU when moving, even if it's hidden by the HLOD systems visibility_range and visibility_parent.



### Steps to reproduce

MRP includes three scenes.
1. 1024 RigidBody3D nodes with static meshes, moving by a constant angular velocity.
2. The same 1024 RigidBody3D nodes, but with the Skeleton3D nodes present. They're hidden by a visibility_parent tied to the static mesh skeleton head node. From the camera's default position, the Skeleton3D are hidden by the HLOD system. Notice the huge performance difference.
3. The same 1024 nodes, with their Skeleton3D in the scene tree, but this time not moving. The performance goes back to green as soon as the nodes land on the ground and stop moving.

Manually hiding the nodes by setting their visibility appears to free up the CPU bottleneck, suggesting that the mechanism that's hiding them through the HLOD system is not hiding them the same way that the visibility toggle hides them. 

It's also worth noting that setting the process_mode of the Skeleton3D and its children to DISABLED does not free up the performance while moving, either - even while being hidden by the HLOD system.

Something about this _feels_ incorrect. Toggling the visibility should have the same effect as the visibility range or visibility parent, and it currently doesn't.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[skeleton-3d-perf-mrp.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21445106/skeleton-3d-perf-mrp.zip)

The resources included are CC0 Skeleton Minion characters by KayKit.

summary: Save external resources before saving the scene

description.text:
`_save_external_resources()` is mainly used to save edited external resources.

Previously, `_save_external_resources()` was mainly called in `_save_scene()`, and `_save_scene()` usually needed to check scene changes before being called. When the scene is not modified, `_save_external_resources()` will not be called even if the resource is edited.

In this PR, after the action of saving the scene is triggered, `_save_external_resources()` will be called first, and then check whether to save the scene. And when `EditorNode` exits the scene tree, `_save_external_resources()` will also be called.

summary: Editor needs restart to show change in debanding project setting
External resource IDs change (and become "duplicates") after upgrading from 4.4.1 to 4.5.beta3

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: 4.5.beta3, 4.5.beta [0dd9178269e447aca805bc4b4a9a840119c10275]
- Not reproducible in: 4.4.1-stable

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (0dd917826) - Fedora Linux 42 (Adams) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 3 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT (RADV GFX1201) - AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 60.39 GiB memory

### Issue description

The preserving of external resource IDs (the `id` part of `[ext_resource ... id=...]`) in something like a `*.tres` file seemingly depends on there being at least one `ExtResource` reference in order to preserve the ID inbetween loading and saving. However, as of #107049 and #107355 we no longer write out properties that are set to their default values, but we still seem to write out the `ext_resource` for things like the type of typed arrays, even for omitted default properties, meaning we can end up with `ext_resource` entries that have no corresponding `ExtResource` reference.

This means that we end up losing the old ID, which forces it to generate a new one on every save of that file. It's only because `Resource::generate_scene_unique_id()` is deterministic nowadays that this ID ends up being the same with every save. However, due to `generate_scene_unique_id` being deterministic, and because we're only generating IDs for these "orphaned" `ext_resource`, you will likely end up with them being "duplicates" of some other `ext_resource` in the `*.tres` file, where the hash part will be the same, but the index part will be different, as seen in the MRP.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Unpack the MRP.
2. Open `my_first_resource.tres` in a text editor.
3. Note the `[ext_resource ...]` entries, their IDs, and the usages of these IDs.
4. Note that `my_second_resource.gd` has ID `1_0g5ot`.
5. Open the MRP in 4.5-beta3.
6. Perform the upgrade from 4.4 to 4.5.
7. Open `my_first_resource.tres` in the inspector.
8. Change the `Baz` property to something like `43` (so that the resource is modified).
9. Save the project.
10. Close the editor.
11. Open `my_first_resource.tres` in a text editor.
12. Note how some properties disappeared, notably the `foo` property (because it was set to its default value).
13. Note how `my_second_resource.gd` is still referenced with ID `1_0g5ot`.
14. Open the MRP in 4.5-beta3 again.
15. Open `my_first_resource.tres` in the inspector.
16. Change the `Baz` property to something like `44` (so that the resource is modified).
17. Save the project.
18. Close the editor.
19. Open `my_first_resource.tres` in a text editor.
20. Note how `my_second_resource.gd` is now referenced with ID `2_8dg0c` instead, and has switched places.
21. Note how that's the same hash as `my_third_resource.gd`, which is `1_8dg0c`.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[duplicate-extresource-id.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21434444/duplicate-extresource-id.zip)

summary: Screen Readers Failing to Properly Interact with Tree Views

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in Godot 4.5 Beta 3

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta3 - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA RTX A1000 Laptop GPU (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6641) - 12th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-12800H (20 threads) - 31.67 GiB memory. Using NVDA screen reader version 2025.1.2

### Issue description

Screen readers fail to properly parse tree views. This is seen most quickly with the file system tree view:
- When scrolling over the file system tree view for a new project, first item that gets announced: res://   data item  res://. There is no indication as to whether this can be expanded with the right arrow, but it can, in fact, be done. First press of the right arrow is silent, and then I get (after pressing right arrow one more time), "icon.svg   data item  icon.svg"
- Likewise, collapsing the tree view, e.g., left arrow on the res:// option, is also silent.
- It is also possible to scroll off this tree view by pressing down (or up) arrow(s) enough times. The latter brings you to the filter files text field, while the former takes you to some unlabeled separator (for me it reads, "   separator  270")


What should happen:
- Typically, screen readers report, when scrolling over, what could be collapsed, e.g., "res://  collapsed  1 of 1  level 0"
- If expanded, screen readers report this fact, e.g., "expanded" "1 item." From a brief test, this is actually 2 separate speech fragments as shown.
- Likewise, when collapsing the tree view item, they report, "collapsed"
- If scrolling over a non-collapsible item, screen readers report this, e.g, "icon.svg  1 of 1"
- It is impossible to scroll off the tree view unless one hits tab or shift tab explicitly (or, presumably, some other hotkey to snap them off to another part of the interface). Arrow keys can never take one out of the tree.


I am aware that was a lot of words. In lieu of this fact, I'm attaching an example of a better (though not ideal) behavior of Python's WX toolkit and screen readers, as I believe it's much easier to mess with it as opposed to parsing lots of jargon. It doesn't seem to report number of items within the tree properly, but it does report expanding, collapsing, and prevents you from arrowing to the close button.


```python
# Tested with wx 4.2.3, Python 3.13
import wx

class TreeViewDemo(wx.Frame):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__(None, title="Tree View Demo", size=(400, 500))
        
        panel = wx.Panel(self)
        
        sizer = wx.BoxSizer(wx.VERTICAL)
        
        self.tree = wx.TreeCtrl(panel, style=wx.TR_DEFAULT_STYLE | wx.TR_HAS_BUTTONS)
        
        root = self.tree.AddRoot("res://")
        
        self.tree.AppendItem(root, "icon.svg")
        self.tree.AppendItem(root, "project.godot")
        
        assets = self.tree.AppendItem(root, "assets/")
        self.tree.AppendItem(assets, "player.png")
        self.tree.AppendItem(assets, "background.jpg")
        
        scripts = self.tree.AppendItem(root, "scripts/")
        self.tree.AppendItem(scripts, "main.gd")
        self.tree.AppendItem(scripts, "player.gd")
        self.tree.AppendItem(scripts, "enemy.gd")
        
        scenes = self.tree.AppendItem(root, "scenes/")
        self.tree.AppendItem(scenes, "main.tscn")
        self.tree.AppendItem(scenes, "player.tscn")
        
        self.tree.Expand(root)
        
        close_btn = wx.Button(panel, wx.ID_CLOSE, "Close")
        close_btn.Bind(wx.EVT_BUTTON, lambda e: self.Close())
        
        sizer.Add(self.tree, 1, wx.EXPAND | wx.ALL, 5)
        sizer.Add(close_btn, 0, wx.ALIGN_CENTER | wx.ALL, 5)
        
        panel.SetSizer(sizer)
        self.Centre()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    app = wx.App()
    frame = TreeViewDemo()
    frame.Show()
    app.MainLoop()
```

### Steps to reproduce

- Create fresh Godot project
- Hit CTRL+Alt+P
- Tab over to the file system tree view
- Use the arrow keys and witness the bug(s)

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

N/A

summary: Severe Visual Artifacts When FOV Changes with FSR2 < 1 or FSR1 + TAA when using shaders

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in godot 4.4.stable branch

### System information

Windows 10, RTX 2070, Pilote 572.83, Vulkan, i7-9700KF

### Issue description

When changing the field of view (FOV) in a 3D scene, severe visual artifacts appear under certain upscaling and anti-aliasing conditions. This issue is reproducible using a minimal project and are related to interactions with the fpsViewClip shader (classic shader technic to always draw view model on top).

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/a6f56f9d-6a05-4e54-ad4f-4073904d1a27

The shader: https://godotshaders.com/shader/first-person-view-model-shader-updated-for-godot-4-3/

### Steps to Reproduce:
- Use FSR2 with a scaling factor less than 1, or use FSR1 with TAA enabled.
- Add the fpsViewClip shader to a material in a first-person setup.
- Dynamically change the camera’s FOV (e.g., zoom in/out).
- Observe visual artifacts (e.g., jittering, ghosting, or pixel stretching).


### Expected Behavior:
FOV changes should occur smoothly without producing distracting artifacts, regardless of the upscaling or anti-aliasing settings.


### Actual Behavior:
Severe visual artifacts occur when FOV is modified:
- With FSR2, only when scaling < 1.
- With FSR1, TAA must also be enabled.

_(The problem appears specifically in combination with the fpsViewClip shader)_

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[bs_visualartefact-minimal.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21416607/bs_visualartefact-minimal.zip)

summary: D3D12: Fix shader model check, initialization error handling

description.text:
Partial fix for #98207 and #98848

The D3D12 initialization code was checking whether the calls to `CheckFeatureSupport()` succeeded, but not the actual returned data, so it would continue initializing even if the Shader Model of the device was too old.

Additionally, if this code were to throw an initialization error, Godot would crash trying to uninitialize partially initialized data, due either to missing checks or bad init/deinit order.

This is only a partial fix because it doesn't automatically fallback to GL, but it at least displays a friendly error dialog (with instructions on switching renderer) instead of crashing, which is better than nothing.

In order for the automatic fallback to work, the Shader Model version check would have to be moved into the ContextDriver, but that requires the `ID3D12Device` which lives in (and is initialized by) the DeviceDriver.

~~I also bumped the minimum required Shader Model version from 6.0 to 6.1 per [this comment](https://github.com/godotengine/godot/issues/98848#issuecomment-2459449786).~~

summary: Blender vertex color imported to Godot incorrect
Focus creates strange behavior with button_down

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in v4.5.beta3.official [4d1f26e1f] and v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6]

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta3 - Linux Mint 22.1 (Xia) on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - AMD Radeon RX 6800 (radeonsi, navi21, LLVM 19.1.1, DRM 3.57, 6.8.0-64-generic) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-7600K CPU @ 3.80GHz (4 threads) - 31.21 GiB memory

### Issue description

I encountered this strange behavior while working on my open-source game [Poder Solar](https://github.com/antimundo/poder-solar), where this issue can also be reproduced. This issue didn't typically occur in the original version of the game, which was created with an older version of Godot 4.

Watch the video and steps to reproduce this issue, which may clarify what the problem is.

The issue is that if you have a `Button` which gets disabled and then re-enabled, and then you try to click it, the `button_down` signal won't be emitted after that first click, it will only be emitted after the second time you click it.
This doesn't happen if you use `release_focus()` before pressing the button down.

It doesn't seem consistent to me at all and it doesn't make sense that if you have focus you can't click.


### Steps to reproduce

As you can see in the video: Open the project, try to click the button until the button is disabled, after the button is re-enabled you will have to press two times to be able to click again.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/26f0f5ea-1a48-4eed-abdf-255a0f6b6428

Script attached to Button:
```gdscript
extends Button

var _money_cost: int = 5

func _ready() -> void:
	button_down.connect(_on_button_down)

func _on_button_down() -> void:
	print("Mango bought!")
	$"../MoneyManager".add_money(-_money_cost)

func _on_money_manager_money_change(new_quantity) -> void:
	disabled = new_quantity < _money_cost
```

Script attached to "MoneyManager":
```gdscript
extends Node

signal money_change(new_quantity)

var money: int = 25
@onready var money_label = $"../MoneyLabel"

func _ready() -> void:
	$Timer.timeout.connect(_on_timer_timeout)
	$Timer.start()

func _on_timer_timeout() -> void:
	add_money(1)

func _process(_delta: float) -> void:
	money_label.text = "Money: " + str(money) + "€"

func add_money(quantity: int) -> void:
	money += quantity
	money_change.emit(money)
	
	if quantity < 0:
		var color_tween: Tween = create_tween().set_trans(Tween.TRANS_SINE).set_ease(Tween.EASE_IN_OUT)
		money_label.modulate = Color("ffaaaa")
		color_tween.tween_property(money_label, "modulate", Color("ffffff"), .3)
		$PlaceItem.play()

```

The problem will be fixed if you add this to the Button script:
```gdscript
func _on_money_manager_money_change(new_quantity) -> void:
	disabled = new_quantity < _money_cost
	release_focus() # just add this line and it will be fixed for some reason
```

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[error-button-down.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21392671/error-button-down.zip)

### Issue description

After entering WebXR, only black is rendered. In the console, this error is spammed:

```
GL_INVALID_FRAMEBUFFER_OPERATION: Framebuffer is incomplete: Stencil attachment has no stencil bits.
```

This appears to be a regression from https://github.com/godotengine/godot/pull/80710

summary: Fonts can get embedded in themes in a such a way that adds long load times

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in 4.5 beta [efb40c152]

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (efb40c152) - Pop!_OS 22.04 LTS on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Mobile) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 7800 XT (RADV NAVI32) - AMD Ryzen 9 5950X 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 62.69 GiB memory

### Issue description

Somehow it is possible for a font in a theme to get converted to a FontFile, and embedded in a theme in such a way it dramatically bloats the size of the theme (by 151 mb) and the exported project, and causes slowdowns when loading. This adds about 8.5 seconds to load on my machine.

The difficulty of trying to re-create this bad theme makes me think there might be a bug in the save/loading code related to this, where dead weight is stuck in the file. Somebody more familiar with the code might know how this can occur.

I definitely never authored it as a FontFile to begin with. Open Sans SemiBold appears to be a font built into Godot so it was not really an option to do it. So the bug could also be related to a code path that converted this font. The project zips incredibly well, and trying to manually author the theme with the same font embedded still results in a smaller theme file. Again somebody more familiar with what actions, if any, can convert this font might know more.

The difficulty in tracking this down also makes me wish there were load time metrics in the profiler, something that just tracked individual asset load times, so you could identify an asset quicker. But that is a separate feature request.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open the attached project
2. Notice how slow it is to boot
3. Notice how large bad_ui_theme.tres is
4. Notice if you delete the OptionButton font override in the bad_ui_theme.tres it reduces the size dramatically
5. Attempt to manually re-create the bad theme by importing OpenSans SemiBold (from godot thirdparty/fonts), assinging it, and making it unqiue. Notice how the size is still much smaller on disk. 

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[bad-ui-theme.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21388933/bad-ui-theme.zip)

summary: Rows in a Tree can become misaligned when using word wrapping

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in: 4.4.1.stable.official.49a5bc7b6 ### System information Godot v4.4.1.stable - Debian GNU/Linux 12 (bookworm) 12 on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Mobile) - integrated Intel(R) Xe Graphics (TGL GT2) - 11th Gen Intel(R) Core(TM) i7-1165G7 @ 2.80GHz (8 threads) ### Issue description When using word-wrap in cells of a Tree, the tree can render in a way that results in misaligned rows, as depicted here: Effectively it looks like the cells with the word-wrap enabled get their text wrapped correctly, and increase in vertical size. However the other cells on the same row are not increased in size in the same way, or at least the alignment line is not moved according to the new size. Once the user moves the mouse over the Tree control the misalignment is corrected and the table looks like one would expect: The same can be achieved by queueing a redraw. Though, curiously the queue_redraw has to be called with call_deferred for it to work. ### Steps to reproduce Attached is a MRP that demonstrates the issue (and was used to obtain the screenshots in the issue description above). It basically consists of a Tree node to which some TreeItems are added (in the _ready function), once the scene starts the rows are misaligned. Take care not to have the mouse pointer over the Tree control when starting the application as it will redraw the Tree with the correct alignment. I assume this happens because the control gets redrawn when a mouse hovers over it (the cell that is hovered over lights up after all). So I figured calling a queue_redraw would have the same effect and serve as a work-around. Adding a queue_redraw at the end of the _ready function does not help, but a queue_redraw.call_deferred does (see the comment in my example code). I had some trouble making an MRP. I suspect the issue happens when TreeItems are added to the Tree when the Tree control is not (yet) visible. An earlier attempt at an MRP where I included a button that adds text to a cell in an already visible Tree did not result in the same issue; the text was added to the cell and the rows in the Tree control stayed aligned. However, in a larger project where I first noticed this issue I have added the call to queue_redraw.call_deferred() to the item_rect_changed signal as a work-around, instead of the visbility_changed signal. So maybe the issue is not entirely related to visibility of the control. ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) [MRP.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21384896/MRP.zip)

summary: Creating a CollisionPolygon2D from a Sprite2D inherits scale from sprite (even though documentation advises never to scale collision polygons).

description.text: ### Tested versions Reproducible in v4.2.1.stable.official [b09f793f5] and v4.4.stable.official [4c311cbee] ### System information macOS 15.3.2 - Vulkan (Mobile) - integrated Apple M1 Pro - Apple M1 Pro (8 Threads) ### Issue description While using the tool in Sprite2D that allows you to create a CollisionPolygon2D I noticed a behaviour that I think is wrong. The Collision polygon that is created from the sprite inherits the scale of the sprite, it kinda makes sense because it also inherits the position, but I know the engine warns in multiple places about not scaling collision polygons because it creates erratic behaviour. I checked the Sprite2D tool (sprite_2d_editor_plugin.cpp) and noticed that it uses the method "clip_to_opaque_to_polygons" in "bit_map.cpp" and in there it's where the scale happens. As I said, I think it makes sense why it happens, probably that method is used in many instances but I think for the creation of collision polygons it should apply the scale to the nodes of the polygon and not to the scale of it. ### Steps to reproduce - Create a Sprite2D node in the hierarchy - Add an image to it - Modify the scale of the Sprite2D node - Select the Sprite2D node - Use the "Create CollisionPolygon2D Sibling" under the Sprite2D tool menu in the toolbar - Press "Create CollisionPolygon2D" in the preview window - Select the new CollisionPolygon2D in the hierarchy - Notice that the scale of it is the same as the Sprite2D node ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) [collision2dissue.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21376495/collision2dissue.zip)

summary: Fix folding node in scene tree dock

description.text:
A small edit that fixes expanding a node folding in scene tree editor.
The problem was calling `set_display_folded` and emitting `editor_state_changed` was collapsing the node but not expanding it.  
  
With this fix, this editor script below works:  
```gdscript
var root : Node = EditorInterface.get_edited_scene_root()
var nodes : Array = get_all_visible_nodes(root)
for node : Node in nodes:
	node.set_display_folded(!node.visible)
	node.editor_state_changed.emit()
```
Here's an animation of the custom tool using `set_display_folded` (folding from node visibility):  
  
![godot_folding_node](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c44125ef-1864-432d-b400-7a4329766759)
